stem: A 68-year-old male presents to the emergency department with symptoms of an acute stroke, including right-sided weakness and slurred speech, which started 2 hours ago. The nurse must act quickly to preserve brain function. What actions are essential for the nurse to take at this time?

itemContext.sbar:
S: This is the ED nurse calling about John Doe, a 68-year-old male who arrived at 14:00. He presents with a sudden onset of right-sided weakness and dysarthria. His wife reports the symptoms began approximately two hours ago at 12:00.
B: Mr. Doe has a known history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He is currently prescribed lisinopril, but his wife is unsure about his adherence.
A: He is alert but confused, with a GCS of 13. Vital signs are BP 180/100 mmHg, HR 88, RR 18, SpO2 96% on room air. A notable right-sided facial droop is present.
R: I am initiating the stroke protocol, need orders for a STAT non-contrast head CT and labs. I recommend a neurology consult immediately.

tabs.title: SBAR
tabs.content:
S: This is the ED nurse calling about John Doe, a 68-year-old male who arrived at 14:00. He presents with a sudden onset of right-sided weakness and dysarthria. His wife reports the symptoms began approximately two hours ago at 12:00.
B: Mr. Doe has a known history of hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He is currently prescribed lisinopril, but his wife is unsure about his adherence.
A: He is alert but confused, with a GCS of 13. Vital signs are BP 180/100 mmHg, HR 88, RR 18, SpO2 96% on room air. A notable right-sided facial droop is present.
R: I am initiating the stroke protocol, need orders for a STAT non-contrast head CT and labs. I recommend a neurology consult immediately.

tabs.title: Vitals
tabs.content:
Time: 14:05
BP: 180/100 mmHg
HR: 88 bpm
RR: 18 breaths/min
SpO2: 96% on room air
Temp: 37.0 C (98.6 F)

tabs.title: Labs
tabs.content:
Time: 14:10
Status: Pending
Tests Ordered: CBC, CMP, Coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT), Troponin

tabs.title: Physical Exam
tabs.content:
Time: 14:05
General: Alert but confused to time and place.
Neurological: GCS 13 (E4, V4, M5). Right-sided facial droop, dysarthria, and right-sided hemiparesis noted. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light.

tabs.title: Radiology
tabs.content: No imaging results at this time.

tabs.title: Care Plan
tabs.content: Initiate stroke protocol. Prepare for potential thrombolytic therapy.

tabs.title: MAR
tabs.content: No medications administered at this time.

b1.hoverRationale: Establishing two large-bore IV lines is critical for administering potential thrombolytic therapy (tPA) and other emergency medications. This intervention directly supports reperfusion of ischemic brain tissue.

b2.text: Obtain a STAT CT scan of the head without contrast
b2.hoverRationale: A non-contrast CT scan is the priority diagnostic tool to differentiate between an ischemic and a hemorrhagic stroke. This distinction is crucial as treatment pathways are completely different.

b3.hoverRationale: Promptly notifying the physician or stroke team activates the hospital's stroke protocol. This ensures rapid evaluation and decision-making within the critical time window to minimize irreversible brain cell death.

b4.hoverRationale: Frequent monitoring of blood pressure is essential. In an ischemic stroke, permissive hypertension is often allowed to maintain cerebral perfusion, but extreme elevations can increase intracranial pressure or risk hemorrhagic conversion.

b5.hoverRationale: Aggressively lowering blood pressure before determining the stroke type can be harmful. In an ischemic stroke, elevated BP may be a compensatory mechanism to perfuse the penumbra; lowering it could worsen the ischemia.

b6.hoverRationale: Physical therapy is a crucial part of rehabilitation but is not an immediate priority during the acute phase. The primary focus is on medical stabilization and preserving viable brain tissue.

b7.hoverRationale: While therapeutic communication is important, it is not a priority action over life-saving medical interventions. Delaying definitive care like a CT scan or IV access to provide reassurance would be a critical error.

b8.text: Wait for lab results before notifying the physician
b8.hoverRationale: Time is critical in stroke care ('time is brain'). Waiting for lab results before notifying the physician or activating the stroke team would cause a dangerous delay in diagnosis and treatment.
